text: <par>Written Tibetan <latinform>ld-</latinform> is sometimes a reflex of <latinform>*ly-</latinform>, as in <xref ref="629">#629</xref> > WT <latinform>ldag</latinform>.</par>
<par>According to Jäschke p. 384, this is a form secondary to WT <latinform>ldad-pa</latinform>.</par>
<par>M1: ;BP pfx? or 229? 1390? or 66? (presume <latinform>(k)a</latinform> because of <latinform>ʔə</latinform>, etc.)</par>
<par>The -y is a learned folk etymology; cf. Skt.</par>
<par>This Lushai (Mizo) form was miscopied from STC #419 in Coblin 1986:48, where it is attributed to Written Burmese.</par>
<par>STC missed the WT cognates. Check da-drag in WSC</par>
<par>This WT form with the archaic <latinform>-d</latinform> suffix (the so-called <emph>da-drag</emph>) is properly to be labelled "Old Tibetan", as in Coblin p. 125.</par>
<par>GSR (p. 316) gives no OC reconstruction, "for lack of Shijing rhyme or Xie-sheng connections."</par>
<par>GSR (give page ref.) gives no OC reconstruction, "for lack of Shijing rhyme or Xie-sheng connections."</par>
<par>Question mark in WSC indicates uncertainty over reconstructed form. See “dream” WSC p. 66.</par>
<par>Coblin (p.92) argues for the semantic connection between this Chinese etymon and the TB root by citing the English expression ‘have a heart.’ </par>
<par>Question mark in WSC indicates uncertainty over reconstructed form.</par>
